wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning the sounds s, a, t and p. Each letter makes one sound: 's' says sss (like a snake), 'a' says aaa (like the start of 'apple'), 't' says t (a short sharp sound), 'p' says p (a little puff of air). Help your child point to each letter, say the sound, then blend them together: s-a-t → sat. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! Short but important. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: I woke up and had to go home through a hole in the fence! I hope nobody saw my pants.

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning the split digraph 'u-e' (as in 'cute'). The 'e' at the end of the word jumps over the consonant to make the 'u' say its name — the long /yoo/ sound. Help your child spot 'u_e' and blend: c-u-t-e → cute, h-u-g-e → huge. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: r-u-de → the 'e' at the end makes the 'u' say its name
u-se → the 'e' at the end makes the 'u' say its name
t-u-ne → the 'e' at the end makes the 'u' say its name
r-u-le → the 'e' at the end makes the 'u' say its name

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! Short but important. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: No, that's not the rule — don't use that rude word! But I was just singing a tune about my bum.

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'ue' is another way to spell the /oo/ sound (as in 'blue'). Your child already knows 'oo' and 'ew' — now they're learning that 'ue' can make the same sound, usually at the end of a word. Help your child spot 'ue' and blend: bl-ue → blue, tr-ue → true. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: bl-ue → 'ue' makes the long oo sound
tr-ue → 'ue' makes the long oo sound
cl-ue → 'ue' makes the long oo sound
gl-ue → 'ue' makes the long oo sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'o' sounds like 'oo'. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: Here's a clue — it's true, it's blue, and stuck with glue! What could it do? IT'S MY SHOE!

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'ew' is another way to spell the /oo/ sound (as in 'new'). Your child already knows 'oo' — now they're learning that 'ew' makes the same sound. Help your child spot 'ew' and blend: n-ew → new, bl-ew → blew. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: n-ew → 'ew' makes the long oo sound
fl-ew → 'ew' makes the long oo sound
thr-ew → 'ew' makes the long oo sound
gr-ew → 'ew' makes the long oo sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! Sounds like 'yoo'. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: You threw your new hat and it flew up high, but it grew wings and landed on a cat! WHAT?

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'ie' can spell the /ee/ sound (as in 'field'). Your child already knows 'ie' as the /ie/ sound — now they're learning it can also make a long E sound. Help your child spot 'ie' and blend: f-ie-ld → field. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: p-ie → 'ie' makes the long I sound
l-ie → 'ie' makes the long I sound
t-ie → 'ie' makes the long I sound
cr-ie-d → 'ie' makes the long I sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! Just one letter but always a capital. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: I ate a pie and that's no lie, but I had to tie my shoe and then I cried — IT WAS DOG FOOD INSIDE!

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning the 'wh' digraph (as in 'when'). The letters 'w' and 'h' together make a /w/ sound at the start of question words. Help your child spot 'wh' and blend: wh-en → when, wh-at → what. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: wh-en → 'wh' makes the w sound
wh-ere → 'wh' makes the w sound
wh-at → 'wh' makes the w sound
wh-ich → 'wh' makes the w sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'ey' makes a long A sound. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: When did they go and where did they hide? Which one of them took my pants — what a cheek!

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning the 'tch' trigraph (as in 'catch'). The letters 't-c-h' together make the /ch/ sound — it's another way to spell /ch/, usually after a short vowel. Help your child spot 'tch' and blend: c-a-tch → catch, m-a-tch → match. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: ca-tch → 'tch' makes the ch sound
fe-tch → 'tch' makes the ch sound
ma-tch → 'tch' makes the ch sound
wa-tch → 'tch' makes the ch sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'o' sounds like 'u'. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: Some dogs play fetch — catch, throw, match! But mine just sits there, eating my watch.

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning double consonant endings: 'ff', 'll', and 'ss' (as in 'puff', 'bell', 'miss'). When these letters are doubled at the end of a short word, they still make just one sound. Help your child blend: p-u-ff → puff, b-e-ll → bell. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: o-ff → double 'ff' makes the f sound
we-ll → double 'll' makes the l sound
mi-ss → double 'ss' makes the s sound
bu-zz → double 'zz' makes the z sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'f' sounds like 'v'. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: I fell off the bed with a buzz — well, I didn't miss the floor! It was made of floor.

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning the endings 'ck' and 'nk' (as in 'back' and 'bank'). The letters 'ck' together make the /k/ sound after a short vowel, and 'nk' makes the /nk/ sound. Help your child blend: b-a-ck → back, b-a-nk → bank. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: ba-ck → 'ck' makes the k sound
thi-nk → 'nk' makes the nk sound
su-nk → 'nk' makes the nk sound
ki-ck → 'ck' makes the k sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! Short but important. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: I think the boat has sunk, so kick it back to land! The plug fell out and it smells SO bad.

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning words ending in 've' (as in 'have'). In English, words don't usually end in just 'v' — they need an 'e' after it. Help your child spot 've' and blend: h-a-ve → have, g-i-ve → give. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: ha-ve → words don't end in 'v' so we add 'e'
li-ve → words don't end in 'v' so we add 'e'
gi-ve → words don't end in 'v' so we add 'e'
lo-ve → words don't end in 'v' so we add 'e'

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'f' sounds like 'v'. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: I have a pet made of fluff. I give him lots of love. Where does he live? IN MY SOCK!

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'y' at the end of a word can make the /ee/ sound (as in 'happy'). Your child already knows 'ee' and 'ea' — now they're learning that 'y' can make the same sound at the end of longer words. Help your child blend: h-a-pp-y → happy, f-u-nn-y → funny. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: happ-y → 'y' at the end makes the ee sound
funn-y → 'y' at the end makes the ee sound
part-y → 'y' at the end makes the ee sound
sill-y → 'y' at the end makes the ee sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! Just two letters. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: I want to be happy and funny at the party! But my silly dog ate all the cake.

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning compound words — two smaller words joined together to make a new word (like 'football' = 'foot' + 'ball'). Help your child break the word into its two parts and read each one, then blend them together. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: foot + ball → two words joined together
bed + room → two words joined together
play + ground → two words joined together
lunch + box → two words joined together

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'ch' makes a k sound. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: At school in the playground, we played football all day with a lunchbox for a ball! Then back to my bedroom — my feet smell. HOORAY!

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning the prefix 'un-' (as in 'unhappy'). Adding 'un' to the start of a word changes it to mean the opposite. Help your child spot the prefix and read the root word first, then add 'un': un + happy → unhappy. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: un + happy → 'un' means 'not'
un + fair → 'un' means 'not'
un + lock → 'un' means 'not' or 'undo'
un + do → 'un' means 'not' or 'reverse'

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! Say it slowly: a-s-k. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: I ask to unlock the shed but it's so unfair — I can't undo the lock! Now I'm unhappy and stuck with the spider.

wordSets.teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'ph' makes the /f/ sound (as in 'phone'). This is another way to spell /f/ — your child already knows 'f' and 'ff'. Help your child spot 'ph' and blend: ph-o-n-e → phone. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: ph-one → 'ph' makes the f sound
ph-oto → 'ph' makes the f sound
dol-ph-in → 'ph' makes the f sound
ele-ph-ant → 'ph' makes the f sound

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'i' is silent. Learn by sight.

wordSets.story: My friend has a phone and took a photo of a dolphin riding an elephant! IN HER GARDEN! (It was a toy.)
